text: The surgeon enters the retro-rectal space using an optical trocar and CO₂ insufflation. The first and second robotic ports are placed, and the initial 5mm port is upsized to 8mm for docking the robot. The areolar tissue in the left retro-rectal space is cleared, with dissection occurring below the arcuate line between the retrorectus fat and transversalis fascia, marking stage one of the procedure.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath approximately one centimeter lateral to the linea alba to avoid injury and maintain its integrity for suturing. In the preperitoneal space, the surgeon navigates between the fatty tissue and the anterior linea alba, approaching the hernia sac while attempting to remain extraperitoneal. The right retrorectus space is entered, and the posterior rectus sheath is incised similarly to the ipsilateral side. Dissection continues towards the linea semilinaris, preserving larger neurovascular bundles, and progresses towards the xiphoid.

The surgeon uses a lateral docking position to perform a right-sided upper TAR, cutting the right posterior sheath to access the preperitoneal space under the diaphragm for mesh overlap. A small defect in the posterior layer is closed with a running absorbable barbed suture. The weakened diastasis extends up to the xiphoid, and the defect is closed by re-approximating the linea alba and incorporating the hernia sac using a number zero absorbable barbed suture. The closure is completed with image inversion using the robotic system, ensuring the mesh will cover the entire space with adequate overlap.

A large piece of medium weight macro porous polypropylene mesh, measuring 25 by 34 centimeters, is inserted through an eight millimeter port without difficulty. The mesh is unrolled and carefully positioned into the retropubic and subdiaphragmatic spaces. The surgical field is then desufflated.
